excerpt: Islam signifies obedience to the Prophet; faith signifies the attainment of peace. Faith cannot exist without Islam.
source_authority: High-confidence scholarly consensus on Ismaili doctrinal definition
source_location_hint: Standard pedagogical formulation across Ismaili literature

excerpt: The Prophet's utterance concerning the sun rising from the west refers esoterically to the emergence of the Imam; outward words prepare believers to advance inwardly toward esoteric meaning.
source_authority: Ismaili hermeneutical corpus on taʾwīl (esoteric exegesis)
source_location_hint: General Ismaili method for interpreting prophetic utterances

excerpt: Faith is perfected through fulfillment of words, knowledge, and belief; covenant is honored through mutual promise between God and believer.
source_authority: Foundational Ismaili legal and theological text
source_location_hint: Principles of faith and covenant in Islamic law

excerpt: The First Intellect stands alone in the sphere of divine unity; the First Emanator brings forth the assembly of all intellects. The First Intellect is the lote-tree; the First Emanator is the Garden of Abode.
source_author: Nasir-i Khusraw / Jaʿfar ibn Mansur al-Yaman
source_location_hint: Al-ʿAql al-Awwal and al-Munbaʿith al-Awwal in cosmological hierarchy

excerpt: God has apportioned faith among all the organs of the human being such that the faith of each organ is distinct from that of another.
source_location_hint: Theological anthropology: faith distributed across bodily members

excerpt: When decline befalls the self, loss inevitably follows in the organ. Those born with defective limbs denied the limits appointed to their presently defective organs in prior spheres.
source_work: Teachings attributed to al-Ḥusayn ibn Jadīn
source_authority: Early Ismaili master of mystical knowledge
source_location_hint: Doctrine of karma and corporeal consequence across spheres

excerpt: The four-hundredth cycle originates from the Ka'ba. The form of the four-hundredth Qa'im is also born from the Ka'ba. Between the first and last stand twenty-eight great personalities through whom spirituality attains completion.
source_location_hint: Doctrine of cycles (adwār) and the Qa'im

excerpt: Al-Sidra (the jujube tree) grows in solitude with no other tree neighboring it. Jannat al-Māwā denotes a garden wherein people may enter and find refuge.
source_authority: Hermeneutical definition of key Quranic terms
source_location_hint: Explanation of botanical and paradisiacal symbolism in Q. 53:14-15

excerpt: The First Intellect stands alone in the realm of pure unity; the First Source possessed a circle of twenty-seven who came under his protection. All intellects merge into the realm of the First Source as the day of the divine month progresses.
source_author: Nasir-i Khusraw / Jaʿfar ibn Mansur al-Yaman
source_location_hint: Structure of the First Intellect and First Emanator

excerpt: Āsher came to recognize that the spirits under his dominion had grown so oppressed that their density could not be rectified by remaining in the spiritual world. He resolved to create a physical world into which the disobedient company could be brought and invited to respond.
source_authority: Doctrine of the fall and invitation
source_location_hint: Cosmological account of the creation of the physical world and the summons (daʿwa)

excerpt: The invitation of the First Intellect transcended time and space; Āsher's invitation was confined within temporal and spatial bounds. As the subtle candle cannot be removed without its dense tablet, so was it necessary to transmute light into physicality to rescue these beings from the spiritual realm.
source_authority: Ismaili doctrine of emanation and creation
source_location_hint: The necessity of the physical world for the redemption of fallen spirits
